children.value:
The higher-order derivatives introduced in section 7.1 are not merely a
mathematical construction. When the independent variable is time, each
successive derivative of a position function acquires a concrete physical
meaning that engineers in every discipline encounter regularly. The first
derivative describes how fast the position is changing. The second describes
how fast the velocity is changing. Each layer adds a new dimension of
information about the motion, and each has practical consequences for the
design of the system that produces it.
You can interpret the first derivative of a position function as
of position as the rate of change of acceleration, called
, and you understand its significance in ride comfort and structural loading.
You can compute velocity, acceleration, and jerk from a given position function and interpret each result physically.
How does the car move after the summit?

children.label: How does the car move after the summit?

children.value:
After ascending the lift hill studied in section 7.1, the roller coaster car
reaches the summit at 40 m above ground and begins its first descent. At the
crest the track is horizontal, so the car’s vertical velocity is momentarily
zero. Gravity then pulls it downward along the descending slope, and the
motion gathers speed rapidly.
We model the height of the car, in metres, as a function of time

children.html: ttt

children.value:
in
seconds measured from the moment the car passes the summit:

children.html: s(t)=40−t3,t∈[0,3].s(t) = 40 - t^3, \quad t \in [0, 3].s(t)=40−t3,t∈[0,3].
t=0t = 0t=0

children.value: the car is at the summit:

children.html: s(0)=40 ms(0) = 40~\text{m}s(0)=40 m
t=3 st = 3~\text{s}t=3 s
s(3)=40−27=13 ms(3) = 40 - 27 = 13~\text{m}s(3)=40−27=13 m

children.value:
, dropping 27 metres in
three seconds. The height zero corresponds to ground level, and the base of
this descent sits a few metres above the valley floor where the track curves
back up into the next element of the ride.
How fast is the car descending?

The instantaneous velocity is the first
derivative of the height with respect to time:

children.html: v(t)=s′(t)=−3t2 ms.v(t) = s'(t) = -3t^2 ~\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.v(t)=s′(t)=−3t2 sm​.

children.value: The negative sign indicates downward motion. At

children.html: t=1 st = 1~\text{s}t=1 s
3 m/s3~\text{m/s}3 m/s
11 km/h11~\text{km/h}11 km/h
t=3 st = 3~\text{s}t=3 s
27 m/s27~\text{m/s}27 m/s
97 km/h97~\text{km/h}97 km/h

children.value: The velocity is itself changing with time, and the rate of that change is the
. It is the second derivative of the position:

children.html: a(t)=s′′(t)=−6t ms2.a(t) = s''(t) = -6t ~\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.a(t)=s′′(t)=−6t s2m​.
a(0)=0a(0) = 0a(0)=0

children.value:
: the net force along the track is zero at the
moment the track is horizontal, and there is no acceleration. As the car moves
down the slope, the component of gravity along the track grows, and the
acceleration magnitude increases steadily. At

children.html: t=1 st = 1~\text{s}t=1 s
6 m/s26~\text{m/s}^26 m/s2
0.6 g0.6\,g0.6g
t=3 st = 3~\text{s}t=3 s
18 m/s218~\text{m/s}^218 m/s2
1.8 g1.8\,g1.8g

children.value:
: passengers feel pressed
into their seats with a force nearly twice their own weight.
We can verify this against the velocity data. Between

children.html: t=1 st = 1~\text{s}t=1 s
t=2 st = 2~\text{s}t=2 s
−3 m/s-3~\text{m/s}−3 m/s
−12 m/s-12~\text{m/s}−12 m/s
−9 m/s-9~\text{m/s}−9 m/s

children.value: over one second. This average acceleration of

children.html: −9 m/s2-9~\text{m/s}^2−9 m/s2

children.value: is consistent with the instantaneous values

children.html: a(1)=−6a(1) = -6a(1)=−6
a(2)=−12a(2) = -12a(2)=−12

children.value:
bracketing it, and the average of those two instantaneous values
is exactly

children.html: a(t)=−6ta(t) = -6ta(t)=−6t

children.value: is not constant: its magnitude grows linearly as

children.html: ttt

children.value: increases. The rate of change of acceleration is the

children.html: j(t)=s′′′(t)=−6 ms3.j(t) = s'''(t) = -6 ~\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^3}.j(t)=s′′′(t)=−6 s3m​.

children.value:
The jerk is constant throughout the descent. Every second, the downward
acceleration increases by exactly

children.html: 6 m/s26~\text{m/s}^26 m/s2

children.value:
. This uniform rate at
which the body force on the passengers builds up is a direct design parameter.

children.html: s(t)s(t)s(t)

children.value: be the position of an object as a function of time. The

is
the third derivative of position with respect to time:

children.html: j(t)=s′′′(t)=d3sdt3.j(t) = s'''(t) = \frac{d^3 s}{dt^3}.j(t)=s′′′(t)=dt3d3s​.

children.value:
It measures the rate of change of acceleration. Constant jerk means the
acceleration grows at a uniform rate. Zero jerk means the acceleration is
constant.

children.html: s(t)=40−t3s(t) = 40 - t^3s(t)=40−t3
−6 m/s3-6~\text{m/s}^3−6 m/s3

children.value:
means that the increase in body force is smooth and predictable throughout the
fall. A large jerk value would indicate that the acceleration changes abruptly,
which passengers experience as a sudden lurch and which generates sharp
impulse loads on the car’s wheels, axles, and track structure. The fourth
derivative of

children.html: s(t)=40−t3s(t) = 40 - t^3s(t)=40−t3

children.value:
is zero, as we expect from a cubic: the jerk
is constant, and the derivative of a constant is zero.

Where does jerk appear in engineering?

children.label: Where does jerk appear in engineering?

children.value:
Jerk is a design constraint in any system that moves humans or sensitive
equipment. In elevator engineering, many national standards specify a maximum
jerk of around

children.html: 2 m/s32~\text{m/s}^32 m/s3

children.value:
during start and stop phases: above this
threshold passengers experience the onset of motion as an uncomfortable lurch
rather than a smooth transition. High-speed passenger trains are typically
designed to stay below

children.html: 0.85 m/s30.85~\text{m/s}^30.85 m/s3

children.value:
in normal service so that standing
passengers retain their balance and seated passengers do not spill drinks.

In structural engineering, jerk governs the impulsive component of loading.
A crane that lowers a heavy component with large jerk generates dynamic forces
that can exceed the static weight by a factor of two or more. The drive system
must limit jerk explicitly, not just acceleration, to keep the structural
loads within the design envelope.

For the roller coaster itself, the jerk profile along the entire ride is one
of the primary quantities that the ride designer controls. A constant jerk of

children.html: 6 m/s36~\text{m/s}^36 m/s3

children.value:
over three seconds falls within the range that passengers
experience as thrilling rather than injurious. The geometry of the track,
through the higher derivatives of its spatial profile, directly determines
the jerk that passengers feel at each moment of the ride.

We have given the first three derivatives of a position function their physical
names: velocity, acceleration, and jerk. For the roller coaster descent

children.html: s(t)=40−t3s(t) = 40 - t^3s(t)=40−t3
3t23t^23t2

children.value:
, the downward acceleration grows
linearly as

children.html: 6t6t6t

children.value: , and the jerk is the constant

children.html: −6 m/s3-6~\text{m/s}^3−6 m/s3

children.value:
. The fourth
and higher derivatives of this cubic are zero.
We now return to the spatial height profile

children.html: h(x)h(x)h(x)

children.value:
introduced in section 7.1
and ask a different question. The first derivative tells us whether the track
is rising or falling. The second derivative tells us something about the shape
of the curve: whether the track bends upward like the base of a valley, or
downward like the crown of a hill. This distinction, which goes by the names
convexity and concavity, is the subject of section 7.3.
